ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn URI for Singaporean steamed rice cupcake; closest match is steamed cake food product (FOODON_03315763).
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CKP (rice-based snack) used as closest defensible match; no exact code exists for Singaporean steamed rice cupcake in FoodEx2 hierarchy.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for this specific traditional Singaporean snack food.

serving.natural_language_portion: One typical serving is approximately 1 piece (40 g), as commonly consumed as a snack or light breakfast item in Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: Steamed Rice Cupcakes — HPB Singapore Food Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values taken directly from HPB Singapore Food Composition Database entry for Steamed Rice Cupcakes (cr_id F11090058). All core macros and micronutrients are sourced from this record with confidence 0.95.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Rice flour-based steamed cake analogous estimate
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. Diabetes Care 2008
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Singaporean steamed rice cupcakes in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at 72 based on the closest analogous food: plain rice flour-based steamed cake / white rice products (GI ~72, high category) from Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care). Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × available carbohydrate per serving) / 100 = (72 × 50.1 × 0.40) / 100 = 14.4 for a 40 g serving.

hypertension.key_factor: Estimated added sugar ~12 g/100 g with very low dietary fibre (0.3 g/100 g); sodium at 166 mg/100 g is moderate but insulin-driven sodium retention from high sugar intake independently elevates blood pressure risk.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit steamed rice cupcakes to one small piece (≤40 g) on rare occasions, as the high added sugar content (~12 g/100 g) promotes insulin-mediated sodium retention and blood pressure elevation independent of the moderate sodium level, per AHA 2018 dietary guidelines.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High carbohydrate content (50.1 g/100 g) with negligible dietary fibre (0.3 g/100 g) and estimated added sugar ~12 g/100 g; GI estimated at 72 (high), resulting in a high glycaemic load per serving.
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should avoid or strictly limit steamed rice cupcakes to one small piece (30–40 g) as an occasional treat, pairing with protein or fat to blunt the glycaemic spike, and monitoring blood glucose 1–2 hours post-consumption given the high GI (~72) and high glycaemic load (~14.4 per 40 g serving).

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Refined carbohydrate dominant (50.1 g/100 g carbohydrate, 0.3 g/100 g fibre) with estimated added sugar ~12 g/100 g; low fat (0.6 g/100 g) and low saturated fat (~0.2 g/100 g) are favourable, but high sugar and low fibre contribute to dyslipidaemia and cardiovascular risk.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should consume steamed rice cupcakes only occasionally and in small portions (≤40 g), as the high refined carbohydrate and added sugar content can raise triglycerides and contribute to dyslipidaemia, despite the low total fat content.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Steamed rice cupcakes are a traditional Singaporean local snack (kuih) made primarily from rice flour, egg white, sugar, and food colouring. They are prepared by steaming the batter in small cup-shaped moulds, producing soft, slightly springy, moist cakes with a mildly sweet flavour and a smooth, tender crumb. The vibrant colours — typically pink, green, or white — make them visually distinctive and they are commonly sold at hawker centres, wet markets, and traditional kuih stalls across Singapore. Nutritionally, steamed rice cupcakes are carbohydrate-dominant (50.1 g/100 g) with very low fat (0.6 g/100 g) and modest protein (2.5 g/100 g). Dietary fibre is negligible at 0.3 g/100 g, and estimated added sugar is approximately 12 g/100 g. The glycaemic index is estimated at 72 (high), making these cakes a high-glycaemic-load food. Sodium is moderate at 166 mg/100 g. Clinically, steamed rice cupcakes are unsuitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes due to the high carbohydrate load, high GI, and minimal fibre. They pose moderate concern for hypertension due to high added sugar promoting insulin-mediated sodium retention, and moderate concern for cardiovascular disease due to refined carbohydrate and sugar content elevating triglyceride risk. They are gluten-free, halal, and dairy-free, making them suitable for a range of dietary requirements. A typical serving is one piece (~40 g), providing approximately 87 kcal.
common_user_queries: Can diabetics eat steamed rice cupcakes?
How many calories in a steamed rice cupcake?
What is the glycaemic index of steamed rice cupcakes?
Are steamed rice cupcakes suitable for hypertension?
What are the ingredients in steamed rice cupcakes?
Is 蒸米糕 the same as steamed rice cupcakes?

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Food Composition Database (cr_id F11090058) with confidence 0.95. This is the primary approved source for Singaporean dishes. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson et al. 2008, which is the approved GI source. Saturated fat and sugar are estimated from standard rice flour cake composition as the HPB record does not separately report these sub-fractions; this is documented in notes.
notes: 1. Saturated fat estimated at 0.2 g/100 g based on typical rice flour and egg white composition from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (rice flour, egg white); HPB record does not separately report saturated fat sub-fraction. 2. Sugar estimated at ~12 g/100 g based on recipe composition (sugar as a primary ingredient) and cross-reference with USDA FoodData Central analogous rice cake entries; HPB record does not separately report sugar. 3. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid profile, micronutrient panel, and moisture data for this traditional Singaporean steamed rice cupcake preparation are not available in the HPB Singapore Food Composition Database or USDA FoodData Central as of 2026. 4. GI value of 72 is an analogous estimate based on rice flour-based steamed cake / white rice products from Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care); no direct GI measurement for Singaporean steamed rice cupcakes exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. 5. All core macros (protein_g 2.5, fat_g 0.6, carbohydrate_g 50.1) are non-zero and sourced from HPB; no zero-macro justification required.
